speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. Let's start by reviewing your fasting blood glucose from today's lab— it came back at 5.1 mmol/L.

speaker: patient
text: Good morning, Doctor. That sounds good—I’ve been trying to keep my diet consistent and walking daily.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s excellent. Consistency really helps with glycemic control. How have you been feeling overall—any fatigue, increased thirst, or blurred vision?

speaker: patient
text: No major symptoms lately. I did notice some mild ankle swelling last week, but it’s improved since I cut back on salt.

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your labs, clinical exam, and history, I’m confirming a diagnosis of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus.

speaker: doctor
text: You’re currently on insulin glargine 10 units once nightly—we’ll continue that for now while we monitor closely.

speaker: patient
text: Okay, thanks. I’ve been injecting at bedtime as instructed and haven’t had any lows.

speaker: doctor
text: Your diastolic blood pressure today is 97 mmHg—slightly elevated, so we’ll keep an eye on that.

speaker: doctor
text: Your hemoglobin level is 125 g/L, which is within the lower end of normal for your age and sex.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been taking my multivitamin with iron, but I wasn’t sure if that was necessary—should I keep doing that?

speaker: doctor
text: Let’s hold off on supplemental iron unless labs show deficiency—we’ll recheck hemoglobin in three months.

speaker: patient
text: Makes sense. Also, I’ve been checking my glucose at home—fasting readings have mostly been between 4.8 and 5.4.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s very reassuring. Your home monitoring aligns well with today’s lab result.

speaker: patient
text: Good to hear. I’ve also been tracking my BP at home—mostly around 132 over 86.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s helpful context. We’ll compare those with our office readings to assess trends.

speaker: patient
text: Actually, that reading you mentioned earlier—5.1 mmol/L—is exactly what I saw this morning too, so it matches that number.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your stable glucose and low hypoglycemia risk, I’m going to add metformin 500 mg twice daily to support insulin sensitivity and reduce hepatic glucose output.

speaker: doctor
text: By the way, your diastolic pressure just rechecked at 89 mmHg—lower than the earlier 97, likely due to relaxation after the initial assessment.

speaker: doctor
text: And your repeat hemoglobin came back at 142 g/L—higher than the earlier 125, possibly reflecting better hydration or assay variation; we’ll confirm with a repeat CBC next visit.

speaker: patient
text: Got it—I’ll start the metformin with meals and keep logging everything. Thanks for explaining the changes so clearly.
